text.tspan: Once a transaction commits,
its effects survive crash, power loss,
process kill, OS panic.
Mechanism: the commit record is fsync'd to the WAL
BEFORE the client gets 'COMMIT OK'.
Even if the in-memory state is lost,
recovery replays the WAL and rebuilds it.
A multi-step transaction is all-or-nothing.
Mechanism: each modification is logged
before being applied. On crash mid-transaction,
recovery either:
u2022 replays the whole txn (if commit record present), OR
u2022 discards partial work (if no commit record).
Never leaves partial visible state.
Without WAL: every commit must fsync the actual data pages
(random I/O u2014 slow).
With WAL: commit fsyncs only the WAL
(sequential I/O u2014 100u20131000u00d7 faster on HDD/SSD).
Data files are updated later in batch from memory.
This is why every serious DB uses WAL
(Postgres, MySQL/InnoDB, SQLite, RocksDB, LMDB, SQL Server, Oracle...).
